title: Alastair Adams -Operations Interview - Bridget | Client Operations Manager - Agency
meeting_title: Alastair Adams -Operations Interview - Bridget | Client Operations Manager - Agency

transcript.text: Good morning, or good evening for you.
Hi, Bridget.
Good afternoon, just about for you, or are you in, I don't actually know where you are, I'm guessing.
I'm on Pacific time, it's no idea.
Good morning then, definitely.
I just started.
Where are you based?
I'm in the UK, so I'm like kind of in the centre of the UK, kind of in between Manchester and London.
Okay, cool, nice.
Have you worked for US?
You have worked for US, I was just looking at your profile, you worked at Handshake, right?
Yeah, did.
Yeah, so it was, I was kind of obviously in the EMEA team, but like I did have to work operation, I had to work with the US quite a bit.
So yeah, I'm kind of used to the time differences, learning where many people live, like learning about different states, because there's a lot of, you know, I mean, distributed workforce, I guess.
So yeah.
Really.
Awesome, I'm excited to meet you.
I was reading through Anne.
And he's notes and looking at your profile and everything.
I'm sorry, I have a cold, so I won't have nasty coughs.
So do I.
I've also got a sore throat.
It's that cold.
I think it's the weekend.
We can just chill.
Okay.
Well, I honestly really have two main questions.
First, I saw you mentioned in your form, you could show me some dashboards if possible.
I did.
Yeah.
Is that possible?
Yeah, I can show you some dashboards if you want to see them.
Yeah, would love to look at it.
Show me something you're proud of.
Okay.
So let me share my screen.
And maybe, and also like, of course, context on like, what was the problem you're solving with it and all that.
Yeah.
Obviously, just for context, these do have numbers in them.
So probably technically shouldn't have maybe.
I won't share.
Copied them exactly.
But yeah, no, no.
Obviously, I just wanted to have examples of stuff that I'd actually done.
And Yeah.

So I've just explored a few PDFs, but this is one that I kind of built from scratch, which is kind of a look into, so I don't know if you, I mean, I guess you're kind of familiar with Handshake, what Handshake is, because it's quite big in the US, but it's obviously kind of an early career platform.
So obviously we had like a product-led data sort of hex side dashboards, which were just like numbers that you could reference to that were like coming from the platform.
But this was more of a sort of a demographic analysis that was specifically for the go-to-market teams.
And obviously at the top here, you can see it is just basically a breakdown of student numbers, like student numbers by institution, by graduation year, cetera, quite basic language proficiency.
Like percentage of like socioeconomic data, but then when you get down to the bottom here, or the lower part, it's quite big, actually, this dashboard, there's also these kind of more deep dive.
Dashboard reports, sorry, that I built, which were like actually to directly inform the sales team.
And this was like...
were you informed?
Like what was their goal with it?
So their goal was they wanted to understand like what's the balance of...
Well, it was actually my idea because like there was the core problem was that there wasn't enough...
Like the employer sentiment wasn't ideal and like it was difficult to nail down like the exact reason for it.
Like why there was like a lack of usage of the platform in some places.
There was maybe the numbers weren't quite there in terms of what they were expecting.
So I thought maybe we just looked at like what's the actual balance that we have on the platform.
So for example, this...
These reports here, like this one, for example, here.
It indicates on a job application that like an employer would put out, like they would do a job description.
They would indicate like where they want their candidate to be based.
And then on a student's profile separately, they could indicate like where they want to work.
So like...
like...
like...
It's just one way here, like you're kind of balancing out what percentage of our students want to work in this location, what percentage of jobs.
Ah, So like Edinburgh, it's like more, there was more posting than people interested.
Yeah, exactly.

So it's like, I mean, you can see London's pretty well balanced, but like when you start to get some of the other cities and you're looking down here, like there's some like, yeah, this one, as you said, there's actually more people that are on the platform.
So sales sees this and what are they doing?
So they're, they're adapting who they're actually, but potentially depending on which side of the sales team it was, they're adapting like who they're actually targeting to kind of account for this.
So like, for example, with some of these ones down here, you could, these are, these are to do with the demand and supply of like the, the majors or the degrees.
And like, so how many percentage of the student population has one degree?
What's the best preference for the job, from the job side of that degree?
So they could basically kind of on the EDU side of the university.

So they could start to kind of maybe target universities that like were focusing on business degrees or like maybe political degrees or STEM degrees and try and acquire a higher proportion of students.
And then like if there was not enough students, but if there was too many jobs, then you could kind of target different types of employers that would fill the gaps.
And that was kind of something that was constantly referenced.

This was actually the most commonly viewed dashboard, the highest viewed dashboard that I built because it was like it kind of became ingrained into the strategy, I guess, of like what they were looking at for.
How was it like how did it get to you, these issues?
Like what's your interaction with the go-to-market team?
And like and sort of I guess this is sort of like what's the org set up?
But like, yeah, how are you getting that information of like what would be helpful for sales to?
Like, how am I getting gaming?
How am I coming up with it?
Like, how am I like how did this kind of whole thing come to life?
I just I just literally I.

We were on a team, it came from just, we were on a team event, the VP of sales, he was just like, Alastair, I've got a problem, we need to just do some data analysis, but he didn't know what to look at, he didn't know how to, he just knew that there wasn't enough data.

This was like a few years ago before we'd like built a lot of stuff, and so I just basically just went home and just like tried to work out like what can I actually build that we've not got, that can provide insights, that can provide like a bit of direction.
And I was quite familiar with what dates we had available, and like what we could maybe make available that we didn't already have.
And so like it wasn't all made in one go, but like it was kind of expanded a little back and forth.
So what was, what's like the stack under here?
This is, this is just Looker, and then on top of BigQuery, so.
Okay.
And, um.
Like, is it all model data in BigQuery?
Are you doing that?
You mean, so, I mean, the majority of this data is just directly from the database.
So, I mean, there's like a student demographic database.
There's like an employer tables.
There's multiple different tables that you can just tie together and have to merge together to create these reports.
But they weren't really, I mean, there was some modeling.
who makes this table?
Sorry, that's a better question.
Who makes the table from scratch?
Actually, there was a data team who did that basically in the US.
And it was all like kind of, I would work with them occasionally, but like it was kind of centralized because the platforms were architecturally basically identical.
So they could, we could just use the same database and the same tables, but like obviously create our own reports for our own needs from them.
Yeah.
What, what other, like, what other data sources do you use primarily in Looker other than BigQuery?
Clearly.
it's really,
For like product, it sounds like BigQuery is like product data.
Yeah, BigQuery is product data.

I guess also flowing into Looker, obviously there was like, I mean, there was CRM data, there was data that's coming from Salesforce, there was data that's coming from Gainsight, there was data coming from Zendesk.
And then, but from the product side, it was pretty much all from BigQuery.
Yeah, was just a decentralized like database that was regional database.
Okay, cool.
This is awesome.
Thank you for showing me.
And then tell me about like, your, the org you existed within, like, I'm curious the structure and like what you were, I don't know, what was like the mission of your team?
So I was, I don't know, Andy, I kind of tried to explain, it's very difficult to explain because it was very, it was actually quite weird when she explained what the growthx role was.
It was very similar to how I, like what role I was doing.
Okay, let's hear it.
So I was recruited originally to do enablement and documentation for the support team, global support team, and eventually the go-to-market teams.
But there was nobody else doing any other enablement role.
There was no one doing any operations.
It was all being originally done in the US, and the time zones was difficult to work with.
So they just basically were like, can you do this?
Learn how to do this?
So I think the best way to learn is to just do.
And so that's how I kind of moved into supporting all the go-to-market teams, still doing all the documentation enablement stuff that I was doing before.
What's an example of like a project there?
I mean, one thing that we did was one big thing that we, I thought there was many, many different things.
But obviously, at some point, we started to expand to France and Germany, like we were trying to penetrate different markets.
So obviously, there was a big project to do with like regionalizing the platform and like regionalizing it.
All the content that we had, like the help center content, for example.
That was a big project.
that like, sorry, it's like customer facing documentation or is it like for the support team to know?
No, it's customer facing documentation and internal documentation.
I mean, obviously the regionalization was just for customer facing documentation.
But then we had, I was also doing Confluence, like internal documentation, like documenting all the processes that we built, like all the data that we had, like indexing it all.
And I guess another example, and I did mention this before, but like it's probably one of the biggest projects was when we automated the creation of all the slide decks for the CSMs.

Instead of them having to like build the data reports or just put, instead of having to go into Looker and like pull out a single metric and put it into a slide deck and then go to another report and find another one, put it in.
And then found a tool and implemented a tool that could connect to the database and then infill the slides.
So.
That you could basically create a whole deck of like a quarterly review or something like that in like two minutes.
It's called Matic.
It's actually really, really cool.
It's a big amount of work to implement it, but like once you've got it going, like it's like, it's literally saving like 95% of time because like you don't have to build these reports anymore.
You just have to put an ID in and it just generates the whole thing.
That would remove so much friction, especially if you wanted to do like weekly updates.
Yeah, you can do so much with it.
Yeah.
Wait, M-A-T-I-C?
M-A-T-I-K.
Okay.
Yeah.
And you can also do things like create one pages, like you can do it for like sales as well, not just for success.
You could like use metrics that you've got and kind of send them out, the current metrics and just generate them really, really easily.
So there's like endless applications for it.
Okay, cool.
And there's some...
Oh, you're a Zapier fan.
Yeah, yeah.
Yeah, I love Zapier.
I used it quite often.
Actually, we created a lot of data triggers with it.
So, like, I would basically connect it to a SQL query.
And then, depending on what the purpose of it was, like, it might trigger a Slack notification when there's a certain thing that's happened.

Like, if a threshold goes below a certain usage threshold, for example, just to, like, flag to the program manager, like, maybe they need to step in and maybe kind of, like, handle something or, like, do something on behalf of the customer, and then they can kind of get on top of it rather than be behind.
But, yeah, I really like Zapier because it's very easy to use, but, like, it's so powerful.
Have you ever tried NA10?
Have I tried what?
NA10.
Oh, yeah, I've heard of I've never actually used it, no.
But it's, like, a slightly more technical Zapier, I think.
Yeah, yeah.
It's, like...
like...
like...
And you should just, I'm just, I would just be curious what you thought of it.
It's like, it's more technical, like you open it up, and it's like, just looks like a beast.
Yeah, I would definitely use it.
It's just like, Handshake had Zapier, they were paying for Zapier, that's what I was using.
Yeah, but I would have used any automation tool, like if it was available.
Yeah.
Can I ask why you left Handshake, or like the transition?
Yeah, they did a big round of layoffs, because they basically started to invest in a different product.
Basically, they've launched a new AI product, and in the US, they're going to do a big round of investment.
And I think the European side of the business was like, slightly not profitable, like it was a bit, it was still scaling.
So like, it was, it was at that point where it's like, not quite broken even.
Yeah.
they just decided, I think like, we need to show profitability everywhere.
So they just laid off like 90% of the staff in Europe, and basically.
Yeah.
Pretty much shutting down the European side, yeah.
Big cut in the US too, I'm just seeing, it was like 15.
Yeah, 15%, like 100, more than 100 people.
Wow, okay.
So that was like, when was that?
Three weeks ago.
Oh, okay.
So you're just on it next.
Well, I mean, I knew about it for a while and I don't want to be sat here like, you know, sort of just not doing it.
I want to be finding something else to kind of...
Yeah, yeah, cool.
What's like, your philosophy on when to automate something or not?
I know that's super broad, like, question.
So whatever comes to mind.

Yeah, I mean, I like to just automate where it can save like menial tasks, like where it can save like useless time, like where someone's otherwise going to have to be doing something that they don't really want to do.
If it's just for the sake of something that someone's only going to do like once or twice, then obviously that's not worth it.
But...

But, like, there's so many things that if you actually, like, review processes and, like, review how people work every day, like, there's just probably, like, 20% of the things that they're doing that they could just be doing way more efficiently.
And you could just kind of be like, well, I could build something or try and help you to streamline that at least.
So, yeah, it's just, I guess it's just situational.
like, I would.
Have you had, like, a good systematic way to do that?
Like, figure out what the heck people are doing every day, you know?
Yeah, yeah.

And, mean, one of the things that I really enjoyed in my last role was being so connected with all the different teams and, like, literally being the person that's kind of at the center of, like, all the chaos and, like, being on top of what everyone's doing and what's the direction of path.
Like, so I guess that's probably one of the most important things in an ops role because you're the one who's kind of supporting everyone and you need to be able to know, like, not just.
Just what they're telling you, but you need to be able to guess what's going to happen in the future and be able to predict where the scaling opportunities could be.
Yeah, that's a good point.
I also find sometimes it's good to make people do something manually first to understand the importance of it or appreciate it, I guess.
Yeah, you mean like kind of show them what they're going to, just to prove the value of them, what you're going be able to do.

Yeah, I think that is important because if you just give someone a process that they've never done and that they're never going to have to do, then I guess they're not going to maybe understand the depth or what's actually functionally going on to actually make it possible.
But most of the time they will have been doing it manually already in order for me to have thought of the way to automate it.
So, um,
Yeah, especially people who are not, like, ops-minded in general, I find, like, they don't realize they're doing something, like, inefficiently.
Oh, yeah, yeah, definitely.
Yeah, if you watch someone's, like, someone's sharing their screen, like, like, most of the time you'll be like, why, like, why are you doing that?
Like, why are you clicking, like, that, that thing instead of using that shortcut?
I mean, you're just a simple thing like that, but, like, it's always happens.
Um, what, um, what are the things you do that give you the most energy?
Like, if you had to pick, like, your ideal, if you could design your ideal workday, like, what would you make, want to make sure you could spend time doing?

Yeah, I think probably the thing that I really like is, is generating insights, like, using data to generate insights, like, that's probably the most satisfying thing, because it's just, it's like producing something from nothing, but there is...
Something there in the background, it's just like never been surfaced.
So I guess that like, I always like to work from data as a basis, even if it is then moving into the kind of RevOps side, but like that's still heavily data-based.
Yeah.
So, yeah, I mean, what I ended up doing at Handshake was probably like 60% data in RevOps.
And then like the rest of it was kind of just like ad hoc, was it things that I'd already been doing for a long time and still had the duties to do.
But that was the thing that really moved into.
That dashboard you showed me, like, are you considering that RevOps?
No, that was more like products and analysis.
That wasn't really RevOps.
Although it did obviously kind of influence the sales strategy, but no, it was more, I wouldn't call that RevOps.
I'd call the stuff that comes from Salesforce, the CRM side to be more RevOps, which none of that was.
Pipeline reporting.
Yeah, yeah.
Like forecasting, like stuff like that.
Yeah, okay, cool.
Who did you report to, by the way?
Like, what org?
So I reported to, originally I reported to the GM, and then they, like, hired for a role of, like, director of operations and customer success.
And then, so that was for the last year.
So I worked under her.
She was, like, she was someone who'd worked at Handshake already, and she got promoted and moved from the US to London to do that role.
It was quite, both ways, I kind of enjoyed it.

It just became a bit more like I was executing more under her, whereas before I'd been more strategic, kind of, like, kind of coming up with what I was doing and just kind of presenting it back to the GM.
But, yeah, I've kind of had both situations, and both were, both worked.
It's just, like, the blend of, like, how much is executional and how much is, like, strategic, the change.
If I asked both of them, like, what...
What things you could improve on?
What would they say?
I'd probably say my willingness to provide visibility to my work.

That was something that constantly came up, like, that I just didn't, that I was doing things, but not making people maybe as aware as they could be of, like, what there was and what my impact was and what I've been doing.
So she helped me to improve that element and, like, kind of just, it's just...
she want to see?
She just wanted to see, like, she wanted to see people to, like, recognise, like, the value of the work that I was doing.
She was like, nope, like, there's a lot of people who, like, maybe who you don't know that well who don't know what you actually do and, like, what you actually, what value you're actually providing.

And I'm not the kind of person that likes to kind of constantly post and, like, constantly kind of be like, look at me, like, well, look what I'm doing, like, that's just my, like, kind of, I don't know, it's just not how I am, but...
It's how I probably assume you should do that.
You should bring more awareness to what you're doing because then people can understand what you can offer and maybe you can help in other areas and you can grow a bit more in that respect.
And obviously the communication element as well.
I'm good in meetings and one-to-ones, but maybe in a big, big group scenario, maybe I'm slightly more withdrawn.
But that's just kind of my personality, I guess.
What do you think, like, what was, what am I trying to say, like, what was she trying to achieve by that?
Like, do you think she was trying to, was she trying to, like, justify the org or, like, what was she, or she just, or she was just giving you, like, personal growth tips?
Yeah, I think it was more personal growth related because there was, like, there was nothing wrong, like, performance-wise, like, with my, like, with my role.
I actually, I don't even, I
I really liked it.
It's funny that this is still here.
Actually, I probably should get rid of it.
But this was an award that I won this year for Move the Marketplace.
And it was the best award that they presented.
What does it mean?
What does it mean?
Yeah, what's Move the Marketplace mean?
It just means it's just a person that's impacted the teams to the biggest extent across the whole org.
So I guess it's like...
Was it confusing then, her feedback?
Was her constructive feedback confusing?
No, it was not confusing.
Because I knew there's things I can improve on.
And she's obviously just trying to find things that will benefit me to grow as a person and to grow professionally.
And she's very much that kind of person already.
She's the person that could just sit in a meeting straight away and you know that she's got the authority and she's got that knowledge and she's got that drive.
But I'm kind of more of the...
The executor, I guess, and like less of that person that's naturally as confident.
But yeah, the work side, yeah, I mean, I was really clearly not doing a bad job.
Yeah, I never thought I'd be in interviews like six months later, but that's the nature of...
Hey, did she get laid off too?

No, she's actually one of the people that's one of the few people, there's like 10, 15 people, I think 10 to 15 people that stayed for some, to kind of keep, like basically to off-board customers that have contracts.
Yeah.
She's obviously director of success, so they have to be someone to do that grim job.
What about the GM?
What would he say?
I think he would say something very similar.
I think he would just say that like, he found me very reliable.
Like, I built the relationship with him just purely on trust, like, and like, delivering.
Like, he would...
not dropping the ball.
Yeah.
Yeah, essentially, I just being, just constantly giving him...

What he wanted and like giving him what he needed and maybe not even what he needed, what I thought that he would need and just sort of making him aware of insights that I kind of came across and things that were going on, like being that person that would kind of feed him that information.
But yeah, I hope he would give, I mean, he would give me a good review, definitely.
Nice.
Hey, that's all great.
Cool.
I know we only have two minutes left and thank you.
I really just was so interested to see.
Like, love seeing the examples of what you can build because it helps me think about like what we could possibly do.
I don't know how Sandy explained, like we're very services heavy today, but it's really just a fund launching platforms to find products to fit.
Like it's a very different approach than typical SaaS, like burn money until you find customers.
Like we have tons of revenue and customers.
So now we can invest like in this on the side, invest in all the like platforms we want to sell.
So have, um,
So like to start, there's just a lot of low hanging fruit on the services side, but it's not as like, there's no, there's not as much data complexity, right?
It's like, there's no usage and like product signals.
So a lot of it's just setting up the foundation of like, how should we track client health?
How should we, we can see our own team uses our own platform.
So like what's going on?
Even just building out like playbooks and like documentation, like you have very well-rounded background, obviously.
And then going forward, I think there's going to be tons of opportunity to do platform stuff.
Like once we have products of like, everything's going to be dumped into a warehouse, like obviously we'll need data eventually too, to like model, deal with that.
But like, then we want to be tracking all the insights of who's using the platforms and how and everything.
So it's like, we're in this evolution.
That's why I think it's a cool.

Cool time to join because you can like really understand the fundamentals of like the marketing world we live in, the growth world we live in basically, and like the service side, and then why we're launching platforms and like have way more context to like what's the value of the platform, right?
So anyways, can I answer any questions for you in the last couple?
I can go like two minutes over, but I do have a call.
I mean, I don't want to take up too much of your time.
I do really like the fact that you, like that both of you, actually you and Andy both described that like that transition that you're trying to go through.

And it is really interesting to be able to actually like define the metrics, like to actually not just use them, but like to actually work out what are the ones that we actually want to measure, like, and then how do we visualize them?
It's going to be like overwhelming to start probably, but I think it's, if you mess that up, then you just end up tracking like so many things.
Weirdly, and it just creates so much debt, essentially.
So yeah.
Thanks.
Like, what exactly is actionable right now, basically?
Yeah, yeah, definitely.
No, I guess the only thing that I would want to know, like, the moment, like, right now, like, how do you actually measure, like, success or failure, like, in the current state of the business?
Like, what are the things that you're actually looking at, like?
For top business metrics?
Yeah, like, I mean, like, just from a sort of client success perspective, like, what would you?
I would say, so obviously, can, it's very simple right now, because they're, like, 60 clients on annual contracts.

So, like, we can look at churn, very easy, churn, we do, like, a onboarding period that's, like, eight weeks, and then they convert into a 12, it's not even really on, it's, like, an onboarding trial sort of thing.

And then they convert into 12 months, so that conversion rate, obviously, so that's, like, it's, like, an opt-out, and then there's churn separate, obviously, we're looking at, like, expansions when people want more.
And then aside from that, would say it's, we've just started trying to put numbers behind it, but there's no tool.
So it's a lot of, it's like, I don't want use the word vibes, but it's like an account score.
Like some of it's like, are we doing the contractual obligations?
Like we said we would deliver five articles a week.
Like, are we doing that?
Like we can check that.
But that's like the quality, super subjective.
Like the relationship score, super subjective.
The performance, can sort of check that because we can, we get access or we can just see their blog performance.
Like are our pages doing well?
ROI is tricky because it's like, all of this is like growth marketing to increase pipeline essentially.
So what can they share with us?
So we know we're impacting ROI.
it's like external reliance.
It's hard when you're an agent, like when you're like services, because you're removed.
There's no platform interface where things.
There's
Pull metrics together.
So today, those are like the five like definitions of account health.
And we're just in parallel while we try to like systematize getting those numbers, we just ask customers, honestly, or like get our team to say and then try to flag the ones that need help.
Um, so it's sort of, and we, we literally started doing that like a month or two ago before, um, this, like the org is very nascent and like a lot of people haven't been in commercial roles before.
So there's been a lot of like coaching.
Also the company's branding.
We've really only had clients.
Yeah.
Yeah.
I think, I think that's the thing that most excites me then.
It's just, uh, it's just the fact that it's like, it's shaping the whole.
It's literally a plank slate.
Yeah.
Yeah.
So yeah, that, that's actually, that's probably the most like motivational thing that you can really have from my perspective, like being able to just have an impact everywhere.
And like, there's just so much potential to do that.
If you're, if you're.
If you're like a proactive, like hand raiser type person, there is no shortage of things to work on.
like, and I always says like, everything is like being zero today.
So you could take anything and make it a little bit better.
Yeah.
Anything.
So it's like, you need to have the eye for like, what is the most, where should I spend?
Because that's hard, right?
And it's like, we do expect people to self-manage quite a bit.
Like, you know, no one's going to tell you do ABC every day.
But there's like, truly, if you were just like catching problems and just like getting  done, there's no way you can like make something better every day.
So that's why I've been liking it.
Yeah.
No, that's, that's perfect.
That's, that's, that's exactly the kind of thing that I'm looking for, the kind of role that I'm looking for.
And you're good with like, we, you don't have to work specific hours at all, but we do ask people to try to stay on until like noon Pacific.
I don't know what time that is for you.
Yeah.
It'd probably be about eight hours difference.
it'd be like seven, maybe my time.

So it's not, it's not too, but, and I, obviously the fact that the, I anticipate that the business is going to go on and will be contributing to the business growing and the product growing, like, that probably And Andy's post as well.
Yeah, yeah, yeah.
So, mean, it's not really a problem.
And obviously I've already been working with, with, with San Francisco quite often.
Anyway, so.
We get such a mixed bag, like a lot of people obviously log off at noon, but then I have like my finance manager, he works, he lives in Berlin.
He works East Coast hours.
Like he prefers to work from like 2 to like 11 p.m.
in Berlin.
He like does a whole lot of But it's very different, right?
He doesn't do that on Fridays, obviously, because that would be annoying.
So, okay.
Yeah, I just want to make sure, like, as long as you have the time to interact with the team and like, interact and like Exactly.
Andy and me and things like that, which I'm Pacific, so I'm not on to like 8 a.m.
Pacific.
Yeah, yeah, yeah.
Yeah, cool.
That's fine.
Okay, awesome.
Well, it's very nice to meet you.

Let me talk to Andy today and then where we have, I think she's designed like a little take-home exercise just to see like what you put together, but also it'd be fun, like interactive discussion at the end of it.
See how that goes.
And then that's kind of the last thing, basically.
So we're trying move quick on our side.
Perfect.
Yeah, I remember the first conversation she said that she wanted everything to be done in two weeks.
That is quite ambitious, but I mean, the speed that you've been going at so far, it seems like you're actually going to be able to do it.
Yeah, and it takes time to ramp and it's like going into the end of the year.
It's like nice to just get someone in now.
They can like sponge for a month and then like get rocking in January kind of thing.
Yeah, yeah.
It does make sense.
Cool.
Okay, well, it's nice to meet you.
Yeah, really nice to meet you, Bridget.
Have a good rest of your day.
Hope you feel better as well.
Yeah, you too.
Thanks.
Bye.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Interviewing Alastair Adams for an Operations role, focusing on experience and fit.](https://fathom.video/share/12PnuafGh6Yvyf3NAp1LyAdPKT13Vu7S?tab=summary&timestamp=317.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [**Data-Driven Impact:** Built a Looker dashboard (BigQuery backend) that became Handshake's most-viewed report, directly informing sales strategy by revealing student/job market imbalances.](https://fathom.video/share/12PnuafGh6Yvyf3NAp1LyAdPKT13Vu7S?tab=summary&timestamp=360.0)
  - [**Automation Expertise:** Implemented Matic to automate CSM slide deck creation (saving 95% of time) and used Zapier for data-triggered alerts (e.g., low usage) to enable proactive customer success.](https://fathom.video/share/12PnuafGh6Yvyf3NAp1LyAdPKT13Vu7S?tab=summary&timestamp=870.0)
  - [**Proactive Problem-Solver:** Self-initiated the dashboard project to solve a vague "employer sentiment" problem, demonstrating a capacity to identify and define strategic data needs.](https://fathom.video/share/12PnuafGh6Yvyf3NAp1LyAdPKT13Vu7S?tab=summary&timestamp=434.0)
  - [**Strong Fit for Nascent Org:** Excited by the "blank slate" opportunity to build foundational ops for a services-to-platform transition, defining metrics and processes from scratch.](https://fathom.video/share/12PnuafGh6Yvyf3NAp1LyAdPKT13Vu7S?tab=summary&timestamp=2012.0)

## Topics

### The Opportunity: Services-to-Platform Transition

  - [The company is evolving from a profitable services agency (\~60 clients) to a platform-based business.](https://fathom.video/share/12PnuafGh6Yvyf3NAp1LyAdPKT13Vu7S?tab=summary&timestamp=1715.0)
  - [**Initial Focus:** Build foundational ops for the services side (e.g., client health tracking, playbooks).](https://fathom.video/share/12PnuafGh6Yvyf3NAp1LyAdPKT13Vu7S?tab=summary&timestamp=1740.0)
  - [**Future Focus:** Support the new platform with data modeling, usage analytics, and insights.](https://fathom.video/share/12PnuafGh6Yvyf3NAp1LyAdPKT13Vu7S?tab=summary&timestamp=1774.0)
  - [**Current State:** Client health tracking is subjective ("vibes"); metrics like churn and expansion are tracked, but a formal system is needed.](https://fathom.video/share/12PnuafGh6Yvyf3NAp1LyAdPKT13Vu7S?tab=summary&timestamp=1865.0)
  - [**Culture:** High autonomy is expected; the role requires self-management and identifying high-impact problems to solve.](https://fathom.video/share/12PnuafGh6Yvyf3NAp1LyAdPKT13Vu7S?tab=summary&timestamp=2040.0)

### Handshake Experience: Data & Automation

  - [**Context:** Handshake EMEA operations; role expanded from enablement to cover all go-to-market ops due to a US-based org gap.](https://fathom.video/share/12PnuafGh6Yvyf3NAp1LyAdPKT13Vu7S?tab=summary&timestamp=780.0)
  - [**Key Project: Student/Job Market Balance Dashboard**](https://fathom.video/share/12PnuafGh6Yvyf3NAp1LyAdPKT13Vu7S?tab=summary&timestamp=420.0)
      - [**Problem:** Poor employer sentiment from low platform usage.](https://fathom.video/share/12PnuafGh6Yvyf3NAp1LyAdPKT13Vu7S?tab=summary&timestamp=434.0)
      - [**Solution:** Built a Looker dashboard (BigQuery backend) analyzing student vs. job demand by location and major.](https://fathom.video/share/12PnuafGh6Yvyf3NAp1LyAdPKT13Vu7S?tab=summary&timestamp=420.0)
      - [**Impact:** Became the most-viewed dashboard, enabling sales to adapt strategy by targeting universities with in-demand majors or employers to fill student gaps.](https://fathom.video/share/12PnuafGh6Yvyf3NAp1LyAdPKT13Vu7S?tab=summary&timestamp=506.0)
  - [**Key Project: CSM Slide Deck Automation**](https://fathom.video/share/12PnuafGh6Yvyf3NAp1LyAdPKT13Vu7S?tab=summary&timestamp=870.0)
      - [**Problem:** Manual CSM slide deck creation was time-consuming.](https://fathom.video/share/12PnuafGh6Yvyf3NAp1LyAdPKT13Vu7S?tab=summary&timestamp=870.0)
      - [**Solution:** Implemented **Matic** to connect to the database and auto-populate slides.](https://fathom.video/share/12PnuafGh6Yvyf3NAp1LyAdPKT13Vu7S?tab=summary&timestamp=870.0)
      - [**Impact:** Reduced deck creation time by 95% (from hours to \~2 minutes).](https://fathom.video/share/12PnuafGh6Yvyf3NAp1LyAdPKT13Vu7S?tab=summary&timestamp=870.0)
  - [**Other Automation:** Used Zapier for data-triggered alerts (e.g., low usage) to enable proactive customer success.](https://fathom.video/share/12PnuafGh6Yvyf3NAp1LyAdPKT13Vu7S?tab=summary&timestamp=960.0)

### Work Style & Feedback

  - [**Ideal Workday:** Focus on data analysis to generate insights and build strategic tools.](https://fathom.video/share/12PnuafGh6Yvyf3NAp1LyAdPKT13Vu7S?tab=summary&timestamp=1286.0)
  - [**Performance Feedback:**](https://fathom.video/share/12PnuafGh6Yvyf3NAp1LyAdPKT13Vu7S?tab=summary&timestamp=1436.0)
      - [**GM:** Reliable, trustworthy, and proactive in surfacing insights.](https://fathom.video/share/12PnuafGh6Yvyf3NAp1LyAdPKT13Vu7S?tab=summary&timestamp=1659.0)
      - [**Director:** Needs to improve visibility into work to increase perceived impact.](https://fathom.video/share/12PnuafGh6Yvyf3NAp1LyAdPKT13Vu7S?tab=summary&timestamp=1436.0)
  - [**Recognition:** Won the "Move the Marketplace" award for the greatest org-wide impact.](https://fathom.video/share/12PnuafGh6Yvyf3NAp1LyAdPKT13Vu7S?tab=summary&timestamp=1565.0)
  - [**Reason for Leaving:** Layoff from Handshake's EMEA team (90% reduction) as the company shifted focus to a new AI product.](https://fathom.video/share/12PnuafGh6Yvyf3NAp1LyAdPKT13Vu7S?tab=summary&timestamp=1043.0)

## Next Steps

  - [**Bridget:** Discuss with Andy and send the take-home exercise to Alastair.](https://fathom.video/share/12PnuafGh6Yvyf3NAp1LyAdPKT13Vu7S?tab=summary&timestamp=2171.0)

action_items.description: Sync w/ Andy re: Alastair; if proceed, send take-home to Alastair
